titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Martha Bradstreet Papers
titlestmt.author: by Matthew D. Mason and Ève Bourbeau-Allard

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Saturday July 27, 2019 at 03:02
Transformed with yale.addEadidUrl.xsl. Adds @url with handle for finding aid. Overwrites @url if already present.

did.unittitle: Martha Bradstreet papers

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The papers consist chiefly of handwritten copies of maps, survey reports, and other legal documents related to litigation by Martha Bradstreet to gain title to land throughout the Mohawk River Valley in New York State. The land, including portions of Cosby's Manor and Utica, was originally the property of Major General John Bradstreet (1714-1774), the stepfather of her father, Samuel Bradstreet. Survey reports and maps in the collection served as evidence for litigation by Martha Bradstreet against owners and tenants of the land, and document landownership in the regions, as well as partitions and subdivisions made during the early nineteenth century within the Mohawk River Valley communities, especially in Utica, as well as Deerfield, Masonville, and Tompkins. The papers also include newspaper clippings, printed notices, copies of court records, and a holograph manuscript of Bradstreet's An Offering at the Altar of Truth (1827), detailing thirty-five cases she brought in federal courts and the unfavorable rulings made by Justice Alfred Conkling (1789-1874) of United States District Court for the Northern District of New York. Correspondence includes letters pertaining to her litigation, as well as personal letters to and from her husband Matthew Codd in the first decade of their marriage (in Series III), and from friend Clarissa "Clara" Bartlett Gregory Catlin (1807-1845) (in Series II).

The papers consist chiefly of handwritten copies of maps, survey reports, and other legal documents related to litigation by Martha Bradstreet to gain title to land throughout the Mohawk River Valley in New York State. The land, including portions of Cosby's Manor and Utica, was originally the property of Major General John Bradstreet (1714-1774), the stepfather of her father, Samuel Bradstreet. The papers served as evidence for litigation by Martha Bradstreet against owners and tenants of the land. Survey reports and maps in the collection document landownership in the regions, as well as partitions and subdivisions made during the early nineteenth century within the Mohawk River Valley communities, especially in Utica, as well as Deerfield, Masonville, and Tompkins. The papers include a holograph manuscript of Bradstreet's An Offering at the Altar of Truth (1827). It details thirty-five cases she brought in federal courts and the unfavorable rulings made by Justice Alfred Conkling (1789-1874) of United States District Court for the Northern District of New York. A small amount of correspondence includes letters and draft letters pertaining to her litigation and personal letters from her friend Clarissa "Clara" Bartlett Gregory Catlin (1807-1845).

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from the William Reese Company on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2009, and from RJM Antiques on the Frederick W. and Carrie S. Beinecke Fund for Western Americana, 2012.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Martha Bradstreet Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Martha Bradstreet Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The descriptions of material in this collection were chiefly repurposed from identifications provided by the dealer.

bioghist.head: Martha Bradstreet (1780-1871)
bioghist.p: Martha Bradstreet was born on the island of Antigua in the West Indies. Her father, Samuel Bradstreet (died 1784), named her after his sister, Martha Bradstreet (died 1782). She married Matthew Codd from Ireland in 1799 and divorced him in 1816. Their marriage produced five children, Elizabeth Catherine (later Bennett), Sarah Mary Anne (later Sterling), Eleanor Cloney, John Bradstreet, and Edward Livius (born circa 1809). Through acts in the New York State Assembly, she regained her birth name in 1817 and applied the surname of Bradstreet to her children in March 1818.

Martha Bradstreet inherited land in the Mohawk River Valley of New York through the estates of her father, her namesake, and aunts, Agatha Bradstreet Evans (died 1794) and Elizabeth Bradstreet Livius (died 1795). Most of the land originally accrued through the estates of her step-grandfather, Major General John Bradstreet (1714-1774) and her grandmother, Mary Aldridge Bradstreet (died 1782). However, poorly drafted deeds of sales created uncertain titles to a portion of the land. The terms of the estate of Elizabeth Bradstreet Livius further complicated Martha Bradstreet's land claims because she had married without the approval of the estate's executor. Bradstreet subsequently discovered that much of the land she inherited from her aunt Martha was sold by the estate's executor, Charles Gould (1726-1806) through his son and attorney, Edward Goold (Gould). During the early nineteenth century she pursued litigation to cancel the land sales and gain title to the properties designated for her in the bequests. In 1831, the United States Supreme Court denied her claim to portions land in Cosby's Manor, a tract of land in and around Utica, New York, in James Jackson, Ex Dem. of Martha Bradstreet, v. Henry Huntington , 30 U.S. 5 Pet. 402 (1831).
Bradstreet continued to pursue her land claims in communities throughout the Mohawk River Valley until her death in Bennettsville, New York.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: Series I. Maps, Legal Documents, and Writings, circa 1750-1877. Series II. Correspondence, 1837-1854. Series III. March 2012 Addition, 1790-circa 1900.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers consist chiefly of handwritten copies of maps, survey reports, and other legal documents related to litigation by Martha Bradstreet to gain title to land throughout the Mohawk River Valley in New York State. The land, including portions of Cosby's Manor and Utica, was originally the property of Major General John Bradstreet (1714-1774), the stepfather of her father, Samuel Bradstreet. Survey reports and maps in the collection served as evidence for litigation by Martha Bradstreet against owners and tenants of the land, and document landownership in the regions, as well as partitions and subdivisions made during the early nineteenth century within the Mohawk River Valley communities, especially in Utica, as well as Deerfield, Masonville, and Tompkins. The papers also include newspaper clippings, printed notices, copies of court records, and a holograph manuscript of Bradstreet's An Offering at the Altar of Truth (1827), detailing thirty-five cases she brought in federal courts and the unfavorable rulings made by Justice Alfred Conkling (1789-1874) of United States District Court for the Northern District of New York. Correspondence includes letters pertaining to her litigation, as well as personal letters to and from her husband Matthew Codd in the first decade of their marriage (in Series III), and from friend Clarissa "Clara" Bartlett Gregory Catlin (1807-1845) (in Series II).

subject.part: Real property -- New York (State) -- New York
Women -- New York (State) -- Social conditions -- 19th century

did.unittitle: Maps, Legal Documents, and Writings

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged chronologically in this finding aid, materials are physically arranged by size and then chronologically.

did.unittitle: Map of land owned by John Bradstreet and D. Campbell in the Springfield Patent, north of Otsego Lake, New York
Extract regarding three tracts of land at Deepy Kill, near Lansingburgh, New York
Patents, survey reports, and division of land between the Susquehanna River and Delaware River, New York, granted to heirs of John Bradstreet
Map of land between the Susquehanna River and Delaware River, New York, granted to the heirs of John Bradstreet
Survey reports and division of land between the Susquehanna River and Delaware River, New York, granted to heirs of John Bradstreet
Statement by Jebediah Sawyer of monies paid to Charles John Evans and Edward Goold from lands sold in Cosby's Manor, New York
Lists of deeds executed by Edward Goold and others for lands in Cosby's Manor, New York
Memorandum designating Samuel Preston to protect and sell land for Charles John Evans, Samuel Ludlow, and Edward Goold

title.part: Patent Granted to the Heirs of the Late General Bradstreet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Printed broadside signed by Charles John Evans and Edward Goold that advertises land for sale through their representative, Samuel Preston.

Original
Copy 1
Copy 2

Copy 1
Copy 2, larger

Map of part of Alexander Cockburn's Gore in Masonville, New York, surveyed by Jonathan Kernsted

Map of the Post Purchase Lot in Utica, New York, surveyed by Charles C. Brodhead in 1812 and altered in 1815
Map of subdivisions in Lot No. 2 in Cosby's Manor, New York, subdivided by Charles C. Brodhead
Map of subdivisions in Lot No. 47 in Cosby's Manor, New York, subdivided by Charles C. Brodhead

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Accompanied by a sale order and receipt for the book and maps

did.unittitle: Map of the northeast part of Lot No. 96 in Cosby's Manor and Utica, New York, owned by James S. Kip

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On the verso is a map of property conveyed by James S. Kip to Davis P. Hoyt on July 28, 1820

did.unittitle: Map of the Evans Patent and Gore, New York

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lands claimed by Martha Bradstreet filled in yellow and land of the Walton Lower Patent filled in blue.

Letter of Attorney from Ann Mason that designates Wearam Willis (1780-1860) to act on her behalf
Map of subdivisions in the vicinity of Hopper Street and Genesee Street, Utica, New York, owned by Maria Miller and subdivided by William Jones in July 1826
Map of land owned by William Butler on Charlotte Street between Steuben Park and Blandina Street, Utica, New York

Map of lands owned by the heirs of John M. Mason and Anna Mason in Evans Patent in Masonville, New York

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Accompanied by an attached report by land commissioners charged by a chancery to partition the estate.

did.unittitle: Map of Lot No. 43 in Cosby's Manor, New York, which shows land conveyed by Martha Bradstreet to the Utica & Schenectady Rail Road Company
Map of Lot Nos. 42, 43, and 44 in Cosby's Manor, New York
Map and notes related to the Gore of Cosby's Manor, New York
Outline map of the Gore of Cosby's Manor, New York
Transcript of proceedings of the Canal Appraisers of the State of New York and award to Charles Gray for damages caused by enlarging the Erie Canal in Frankfort, New York
Portion of printed map that shows land patents in Delaware County, New York
Diagram of the subdivision of Lot No. 91 of Cosby's Manor in Bainbridge, New York, by E. P. Bennett
Memoranda of facts related to Martha Bradstreet v. Charles Gray
Sketch of Lot No. 44 in the Evans Patent, New York, by Marshal Hulce for Edward Livius Bradstreet
Outline map of an irregularly shaped land lot
Correspondence
Clarissa "Clara" Bartlett Gregory Catlin (1807-1845) to Martha Bradstreet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three letters that document their friendship as well as debts that she and her husband, American painter and author George Catlin (1796-1872), owe Bradstreet, and vice versa. A letter mentions portraits Catlin offered Lewis Beck, professor of geology at Rutgers University. A promissory note for five dollars from Bradstreet to George Catlin accompanies the letters.

did.unittitle: Martha Bradstreet to "Sir"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Documents her journey from Albany, New York, to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

did.unittitle: Martha Bradstreet to Canal Appraisers of the State of New York

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A draft letter that details her claim for damages incurred to her property by the Erie Canal in Frankfort, New York

did.unittitle: Martha Bradstreet to stock holders

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A draft letter that details her property claims to stockholders of an unidentified construction project on Lot No. 90 of Cosby's Manor, New York

did.unittitle: Martha Bradstreet to Henry Augustus Muhlenberg (1823-1854)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Details her continuing litigation and news of shared acquaintances. Muhlenberg died on January 9, 1854, so Bradstreet consequently did not send the letter.

did.unittitle: March 2012 Addition

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The March 2012 Addition contains correspondence, copies of court records and legal documents, newspaper clippings, and other material relating to Martha Bradstreet's litigation for land, especially in Evans Patent, Masonville, Delaware County, and to her finances. Individuals represented in correspondence, briefs and receipts include Aaron Burr Jr., her sons John and Edward L. Bradstreet, newspaper editor Edwin Croswell, lawyers Thomas Addis Emmet, Ebenezer Foote, and John L. Tillinghast, sheriff Roger Case of Delaware County, and merchant Daniel Ludlow. Also included is correspondence between Bradstreet and her husband Matthew Codd circa 1799-1808 documenting their family life and relationship, as well as exemplifications of court rulings on their divorce.

did.unittitle: Indentures for land sales

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Seven indentures for the sale of a lot in Montgomery County, NY to Stephen Potter, and Cosby's Manor Lot Nos. 91, 95, 96, in Herkimer County, NY to various buyers.

scopecontent.p: Accompanied by a public notice regarding the will, dated 1827 September 15.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Accompanied by two transcriptions of deeds regarding Evans Patent in Delaware County, New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also includes a clipping of a letter to the editor by John L. Tillinghast published in 1829.

did.unittitle: Letters from Martha Bradstreet to the editors of the Albany Daily Advertiser

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also includes a clipping of an anonymous letter to the editor concerning Martha Bradstreet published in 1832.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
